title: DHCP DORA Process
examTip: DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client). DORA is: Discover (Broadcast) -> Offer (Unicast/Broadcast) -> Request (Broadcast) -> Ack (Unicast/Broadcast). Source IP is 0.0.0.0 during Discover and Request because the client has no IP assigned yet.
bottomTitle: DHCP DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge) Process Flow

messages.label: 1. DHCP DISCOVER (Broadcast)
messages.fromNote:
Src IP: 0.0.0.0
Dst IP: 255.255.255.255
Src MAC: Client_MAC
Dst MAC: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF
messages.toNote:
Listens on UDP 67
Receives Discover
from Client MAC

messages.label: 2. DHCP OFFER (Unicast/Broadcast)
messages.fromNote:
Selects IP: 192.168.1.100
Subnet Mask: 255.255.255.0
Gateway: 192.168.1.1
Lease Time: 8 Hours
messages.toNote:
Src IP: 192.168.1.1
Dst IP: 192.168.1.100 / 255.255.255.255
Src MAC: Server_MAC
Dst MAC: Client_MAC / FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF

messages.label: 3. DHCP REQUEST (Broadcast)
messages.fromNote:
Src IP: 0.0.0.0
Dst IP: 255.255.255.255
Requests 192.168.1.100
Identifies Server IP

messages.label: 4. DHCP ACK (Unicast/Broadcast)
messages.fromNote:
Commits IP lease
Sends final confirmation
with options
messages.toNote:
Src IP: 192.168.1.1
Dst IP: 192.168.1.100 / 255.255.255.255
Applies IP config
State: BOUND

notes: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) operates over UDP on ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).
Discover and Request messages must be broadcasted because the client has no assigned IP address yet.
Offer and Ack messages can be unicast or broadcast depending on the network stack and client hardware capabilities.
Lease Renewal: At 50% of the lease duration (T1 timer), client sends unicast DHCPREQUEST to renew. If no response, at 87.5 (T2 timer) it broadcasts to find any server.
theory:
Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) is an Application Layer protocol that dynamically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters (subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers) to devices on a network. It operates on a client-server model using UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

The DORA process is the four-step handshake used by a DHCP client to acquire network configurations:

1. Discover (Broadcast): The client, having no IP address, broadcasts a DHCPDISCOVER message on the local network (destination IP: 255.255.255.255, source IP: 0.0.0.0, destination MAC: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) searching for any active DHCP server.

2. Offer (Unicast/Broadcast): Any DHCP server receiving the Discover message responds with a DHCPOFFER message. This contains an available IP address from the server's pool (scope), subnet mask, lease duration, default gateway IP, and DNS server IPs.

3. Request (Broadcast): The client selects one offer (typically the first received) and broadcasts a DHCPREQUEST message. This informs the selected server of acceptance and implicitly notifies other DHCP servers to release their offered IPs back to their pools.

4. Acknowledge (Broadcast/Unicast): The selected server receives the Request and responds with a DHCPACK message, committing the IP binding and configuration parameters to the client. Upon receiving the ACK, the client configures its network interface and transitions to the BOUND state.
keyPoints: DHCP dynamically configures TCP/IP settings: IP, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers.
Uses client-server architecture with UDP port 67 (server) and UDP port 68 (client).
Four-step DORA process: Discover (Broadcast), Offer (Unicast/Broadcast), Request (Broadcast), Ack (Unicast/Broadcast).
Lease model: IP addresses are rented for a lease time; client must renew using T1 (50%) and T2 (87.5%) timers.
DHCP relay agents allow a single DHCP server to service multiple subnets by forwarding broadcast traffic as unicast.
useCases: Automating IP configuration in home and corporate networks to prevent administrative overhead and IP conflicts.
Mobile client management in public hotspots (cafes, airports) where users connect and disconnect frequently.
